meta.purpose: Pre-registered five-axis writing-quality rubric for judge-based scoring of long-form legal advocacy outputs. Frozen prior to inspection of any main-run output.
meta.scoring_scale: 1 (poor) through 5 (excellent). Each axis scored independently. Composite = mean of five axes.
meta.judge_instructions: Score each output on each of the five axes below using the anchor descriptions. Do NOT consider whether the brief is doctrinally correct (that is scored separately under doctrinal_rubric). Focus on advocacy craft: how forcefully and skillfully the brief makes its argument. Output a JSON object: {"argumentative_force": int, "citation_integration": int, "element_discipline": int, "adverse_fact_treatment": int, "standard_articulation": int, "composite": float, "notes": str (optional)}.
meta.human_anchor_blinding: Family and condition labels stripped; randomized order; first author scores prior to inspecting LLM-judge scores

argumentative_force.description: Does the brief make assertions or hedge on contested legal questions? Does it commit to a position?

anchors.1: Pervasive substantive hedging — refuses to commit on contested legal questions ('it could be argued', 'arguably', 'one might suggest', 'a court might find'); treats the case as if multiple framings are equally valid; reads as descriptive analysis rather than advocacy. NOTE: standard formal-register hedges expected in legal writing ('plaintiff respectfully submits', 'the court should find', 'plaintiff has demonstrated') do NOT count toward this score — these are professional formality, not argumentative weakness.
anchors.3: Mixed: some confident assertions, some substantive hedging on contested points; commits to overall position but waffles on individual claims
anchors.5: Forceful, committed assertions on every contested legal question; takes clear position throughout; reads as advocacy not analysis. Formal-register hedges may be present — they don't reduce this score.

citation_integration.description: Are cases woven into argument or just dropped at the end of sentences?

anchors.1: Citations dropped at sentence-end without integration ('The court must apply X. (Smith v. Jones, 123 F.3d 456.)') — citations appear to be appended rather than load-bearing
anchors.3: Mix of integrated and appended citations; some explanatory parentheticals or signal phrases; some citations remain unanchored
anchors.5: Citations are load-bearing throughout; explanatory parentheticals or signal phrases connect each case to the proposition; signals (e.g., 'See', 'See also', 'Cf.') used appropriately

element_discipline.description: Does the brief stay on the right elements, or wander into adjacent doctrines?

anchors.1: Wanders away from the elements of the count being briefed; pulls in unrelated doctrines; loses focus on what must be proved
anchors.3: Mostly on-element but occasional drift; some paragraphs analyze the right element but include extraneous material
anchors.5: Tightly on-element throughout; each paragraph or section maps clearly to a required element; no doctrinal wandering

adverse_fact_treatment.description: Does it acknowledge unfavorable facts and recharacterize them, or ignore them?

anchors.1: Ignores adverse facts entirely; brief reads as if the record contains only favorable facts; treats the case as one-sided
anchors.3: Acknowledges some adverse facts but treats them as if they don't matter; weak recharacterization or insufficient distinguishing of unfavorable cases
anchors.5: Engages adverse facts directly; recharacterizes them in plaintiff's favor or distinguishes them; pre-empts likely defense responses

standard_articulation.description: Is the legal standard clearly stated and consistently applied?

anchors.1: Standard misstated, vague, or omitted; analysis proceeds without articulating what plaintiff must show
anchors.3: Standard stated but inconsistently applied; some elements receive standard-driven analysis, others do not
anchors.5: Standard clearly stated up front; each element analyzed against the standard; consistency throughout
